summary:
The Nguyễn Dynasty (1802–1945 AD) was Vietnam's last imperial dynasty, founded by Nguyễn Ánh (Emperor Gia Long) who unified Vietnam from north to south for the first time in centuries after defeating the [[event:2517|Tây Sơn]] rebels with French military assistance. Nguyễn Ánh's alliance with French Bishop Pigneau de Béhaine brought French officers, engineers, and weapons that modernized his army — creating a debt and precedent of French involvement that would eventually lead to French colonization. After his victory, Gia Long established Huế as his capital (midway between the north and south), adopted Chinese-style imperial titles, and named his new unified realm 'Vietnam' — a name the Qing court initially contested as too grandiose.

The early Nguyễn dynasty was culturally conservative: it prioritized Confucian orthodoxy, built an elaborate imperial palace complex at Huế modeled on the Forbidden City, and initially limited Catholic missionary activity despite owing its existence partly to French Catholic assistance — a contradiction that generated tensions. Emperor Minh Mạng (r. 1820–1841) imposed uniformity on the diverse kingdom, suppressed regional autonomy, and persecuted Christians — providing the pretext for French military intervention. The bombardment of Đà Nẵng in 1858 began the French colonial conquest that made Vietnam part of French Indochina by 1884, reducing the Nguyễn emperors to French-controlled figureheads ruling from Huế until the abdication of Bảo Đại in August 1945 — four days after the atomic bombing of Nagasaki.
